title: Skip INT3 Stepping does not work on current builds
author: colinsenner
body:
### Operating System

Windows 11 Pro 25H2 OS 26200.8737

### x64dbg Version

May 27, 2026

### Describe the issue

TLDR: Skip IN3 Stepping is not working when I attempt to step over an int3 instruction. 

I tested the two latest builds which both have the problem, Aug 19th, 2025 does not have the issue.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start a binary
2. Run until you hit an int3
3. Step over another int3
4. You will not be able to continue stepping after this 


### Attachments

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/78c29287-2692-4ca1-986c-37dac4e5f9bd

title: Crash in Qt accessibility path (x64gui+0xBD90C / x32gui+0xA40E2)
author: BikonLI
body:
### Operating System

Windows 11 Home China x64.

Local system information:

- `Get-CimInstance Win32_OperatingSystem` reports `Version=10.0.26200`, `BuildNumber=26200`.
- Windows Error Reporting dynamic signature reports `OS Version=10.0.26200.2`.
- WER `OsInfo[29]` reports `10.0.26100.8655.amd64fre.ge_release.240331-1435`.
- Locale ID: `2052`.

### x64dbg Version

`snapshot_2026-05-27_12-11`, file version `0.0.2.5`.

Matching symbols package `symbols-snapshot_2026-05-27_12-11.zip` has `commithash.txt`:

```text
9c8ca1cae0b6d56cc44f31fddcb10e3b02ffbb87
```

### Describe the issue

x64dbg repeatedly crashes in the GUI module with an access violation. The crash happens in the x64 GUI build, and a similar crash was also observed in the x32 GUI build.

Latest x64 WER crash signature:

```text
Faulting application: x64dbg.exe
Application version: 0.0.2.5
Application timestamp: 0x6a16df14
Faulting module: x64gui.dll
Faulting module timestamp: 0x6a16df0f
Exception code: 0xc0000005
Exception offset: 0x00000000000bd90c
```

Latest x32 WER crash signature:

```text
Faulting application: x32dbg.exe
Application version: 0.0.2.5
Application timestamp: 0x6a16de99
Faulting module: x32gui.dll
Faulting module timestamp: 0x6a16de97
Exception code: 0xc0000005
Exception offset: 0x000a40e2
```

Both reports loaded Windows accessibility / UI automation modules:

```text
Oleacc.dll
UIAutomationCore.dll
```

The x64 crash report also had `plugins\ScyllaHideX64DBGPlugin.dp64` loaded, but the x32 crash report did not load ScyllaHide and still crashed in `x32gui.dll`, so ScyllaHide does not appear to be required to hit this crash.

I downloaded the matching symbols package and checked the code around both offsets. The x64 and x32 crashes appear to be in the same Qt accessibility update path.

At `x64gui.dll+0xBD90C`, the nearby imports/calls include:

```text
QAccessible::isActive()
QAccessible::queryAccessibleInterface(QObject*)
QAccessibleValueChangeEvent::QAccessibleValueChangeEvent(QAccessibleInterface*, QVariant const&)
QAccessible::updateAccessibility(QAccessibleEvent*)
```

The immediate x64 crash point is an unchecked dereference after an accessibility interface lookup / virtual call:

```asm
call    QAccessible::queryAccessibleInterface(QObject*)
...
call    qword ptr [rax+40h]
mov     rsi, rax
mov     rcx, qword ptr [rax]   ; AV at x64gui.dll+0xBD90C when rax is invalid/null
```

The x32 crash at `x32gui.dll+0xA40E2` follows the analogous path:

```asm
call    QAccessible::isActive
call    QAccessible::queryAccessibleInterface(QObject*)
...
mov     ecx, ebx
mov     edx, dword ptr [ebx]   ; AV at x32gui.dll+0xA40E2 when ebx is invalid/null
```

This looks like an x64dbg GUI accessibility bug triggered when Windows UI Automation / accessibility clients query the UI. The expected behavior would be that accessibility notifications do not crash even if `queryAccessibleInterface` or a child interface lookup returns null/invalid.

### Steps to reproduce

I do not have a minimal deterministic repro yet, but this is the observed environment and behavior:

1. Run `snapshot_2026-05-27_12-11` on the Windows 11 system described above.
2. Launch x64dbg or x32dbg and use it normally. No obvious screen reader is running, but WER shows `Oleacc.dll` and `UIAutomationCore.dll` loaded at crash time.
3. x64dbg eventually crashes by itself with the WER signature above. x32dbg has also crashed in the analogous GUI/accessibility path.

### Attachments

No dump is available right now. I can provide the full WER report text if useful. The key WER fields and offset analysis are included above.

title: Add Keyboard shortcut for the same action of clicking the green/red dot next to a breakpoint
author: Ivanca
body:
### Feature type

Quality of life

### Describe the feature

The action of clicking a green/red dot next to breakpoint should have a keyboard shortcut (or a way to assign one), initially I thought this was called "toggling the breakpoint" but that is something different, because doing that deletes the breakpoint instead, here I'm referring exclusively to the green/red state toggle that happens when the dot is clicked (an action that does not delete any data such as the conditional of the breakpoint, the name, the log text or any of the other fields)

Keyboard shortcut to do this exact same action:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6a5dea33-393d-475c-b5ed-850e96fa1ac0

Windows 10 64bit, Build 18363

May 27 2026, 12:09:19

Pressing the context menu "Breakpoint > toggle" option should do the same that pressing the red dot that symbolizes a breakpoint, meaning going from on to off and back, it should not delete the breakpoint completely like it does now. For actual deletion I believe the best approach is to have a new option inside the breakpoint menu called "Delete"

2. Select `Breakpoint > toggle`
3. See that is deleted

title: Copy/Ctrl+C from Disassembly address column should output full address regardless of column width
author: 52only
body:
### Operating System

win10

### x64dbg Version

May 27 2026

### Describe the issue

Example:
- Actual address: `00401000`
- Address column narrowed to show only `00401` → copied text is `00401` (missing trailing chars)

This is misleading and can cause errors when pasting addresses elsewhere (e.g. into scripts, notes, or calculator).

Middle-click on the address column does copy the full address correctly, but standard `Copy` / `Ctrl+C` on selected lines does not.

When copying disassembly lines (or specifically the address column), always use the **full formatted virtual address** (e.g. `00401000` for x32, `00007FF6A2B41000` for x64) regardless of the visible column width.


Thanks!

### Steps to reproduce

1. 
2. 
3.

title: Please add a new feature
author: 52only
body:
### Feature type

Other

### Describe the feature

Currently the Hex Dump window right-click menu has no option to interpret data as PE-related structures (IMAGE_DOS_HEADER, IMAGE_NT_HEADERS32/64, IMAGE_SECTION_HEADER, etc.). Users must manually calculate offsets (e.g. `e_lfanew` at +0x3C) to locate the PE signature and headers, which is inconvenient compared to OllyDbg's "PE Header" display.

title: Feature request: plugin mechanism for observing realtime log messages
author: dariushoule
body:
### Summary
There is currently no way for a plugin to observe x64dbg's log output through the plugin SDK. `GuiLogRedirect` and `GuiLogSave` are close to being viable mechanisms for log observation but have the following pitfalls:

- `GuiLogRedirect` retains exclusive rights to the created file, so plugins cannot simultaneously open it for read. Its possible writing to a named pipe may be viable but I don't think it was built with those semantics in mind.
- `GuiLogSave` does not give the ability to trivially tail the latest logs, only the whole logging window, which is prohibitively heavy.
- `GuiLogSave` and `GuiLogRedirect` appear to be racy, there's no way to know when lines displayed in the UI are going to be made available in downstream files. IIUC a way to manually flush, or turn off buffering would be required.
- `GuiLogRedirect` diverts, but another desired solution would be a tee. Being able to provide both an operator and scripts/MCP logs is ideal.

### Motivation
([x64dbg-automate](https://github.com/dariushoule/x64dbg-automate)) drives the debugger UI through python scripting and MCP interfaces. Currently taking an action and observing x64dbg logs is not implemented, and is a desired feature.

See:
> A lot of times I'm using my lib's MCP side-by-side while working with the UI. Giving it the ability to take actions and also see the same logs as me in real time would be nice.

title: Crash: `__debugbreak()` in `_dbg_sendmessage` during shutdown
author: justanotheranonymoususer
body:
### Operating System

Win11 10.0.26200.8457

### x64dbg Version

Apr 20 2026

### Describe the issue

I just closed the window while a debuggee is loaded. Manually reviewed AI analysis below.

## Summary

| | |
|---|---|
| Exception | `EXCEPTION_BREAKPOINT` (`0x80000003`), last chance |
| Faulting address | `x64dbg.00007FF913FBC13E` = `_dbg_sendmessage+5E` |
| Real cause | Deliberate `__debugbreak()` assertion, not a stray breakpoint |
| Message type | `DBG_GET_STRING_AT` (`0x26`) |
| Trigger | A Qt `paintEvent` called `DbgGetStringAt` after the engine was stopped |
| Class of bug | Shutdown race between the GUI paint loop and the close worker thread |

In a release build the unconditional `__debugbreak()` terminates the whole process.

## The crash site

The `int3` the dump landed on is the `default` case of the first switch in `_dbg_sendmessage`:

```cpp
// src/dbg/_exports.cpp:900
duint _dbg_sendmessage(DBGMSG type, void* param1, void* param2)
{
    if(dbgisstopped())
    {
        switch(type) //ignore win events
        {
        //these functions are safe to call when we did not initialize yet
        case DBG_DEINITIALIZE_LOCKS:
        case DBG_INITIALIZE_LOCKS:
        case DBG_GET_FUNCTIONS:
        case DBG_SETTINGS_UPDATED:
        case DBG_GET_THREAD_LIST:
        case DBG_WIN_EVENT:
        case DBG_WIN_EVENT_GLOBAL:
        case DBG_RELEASE_ENCODE_TYPE_BUFFER:
        case DBG_GET_TIME_WASTED_COUNTER:
        case DBG_GET_DEBUG_ENGINE:
            break;
        //the rest is unsafe -> throw an exception when people try to call them
        default:
            __debugbreak(); // <-- crash: _dbg_sendmessage+5E
        }
    }
    ...
```

`dbgisstopped()` returns `bIsStopped`, which is:

- `true` initially,
- set `false` at the end of `_dbg_dbginit()` (`src/dbg/x64dbg.cpp:936`),
- set `true` again as the **last** statement of `_dbg_dbgexitsignal()` (`src/dbg/x64dbg.cpp:983`).

## Matching the disassembly

The compiler turned the `switch` into a jump table. With `type = DBG_GET_STRING_AT = 0x26`:

```
00007FF913FBC10E  call x64dbg.7FF913EB7AE0          ; dbgisstopped()
00007FF913FBC11A  test al,al
00007FF913FBC11C  je   7FF913FBC13F                 ; not stopped -> skip to main switch
00007FF913FBC11E  lea  eax,[rsi-10]                 ; rsi = type = 0x26 -> eax = 0x16
00007FF913FBC121  cmp  eax,40
00007FF913FBC124  ja   7FF913FBC13E                 ; 0x16 <= 0x40 -> NOT taken
00007FF913FBC126  cdqe
00007FF913FBC128  movzx eax,byte ptr [r8+rax+11D914] ; byte index table[0x16] -> default group
00007FF913FBC131  mov  ecx,[r8+rax*4+11D90C]        ; dword offset table -> offset of int3
00007FF913FBC139  add  rcx,r8
00007FF913FBC13C  jmp  rcx                          ; -> 7FF913FBC13E
00007FF913FBC13E  int3                              ; __debugbreak(): _dbg_sendmessage+5E
```

`DBG_GET_STRING_AT` (`0x26`) falls inside the table's covered range `[0x10, 0x50]` (upper bound exists because the highest allow-listed value, `DBG_GET_DEBUG_ENGINE`, is `0x4B`). Because it is not in the allow-list, the table routes it straight to the `default: __debugbreak()`. The dump's `type = DBG_GET_STRING_AT` matches both the caller frame and this arithmetic exactly.

## Call stack (logical chain)

Bottom to top, this is a Qt repaint reaching into the debugger engine:

```
QEventDispatcherWin32::processEvents
  DispatchMessageW
    UserCallWinProcCheckWow
      QCoreApplicationPrivate::sendPostedEvents
        QApplication::notify / notify_helper
          QWidget::event                       (paint)
            QWidgetPrivate::drawWidget
              QWidgetPrivate::paintSiblingsRecursive   (deep recursion)
                ... custom x64gui widget paintEvent ...
                  DbgGetStringAt                 (x64bridge)
                    _dbg_sendmessage(DBG_GET_STRING_AT)   --> __debugbreak()
```

`DbgGetStringAt` is a thin wrapper:

```cpp
// src/bridge/bridgemain.cpp:1422
BRIDGE_IMPEXP bool DbgGetStringAt(duint addr, char* text)
{
    if(_dbg_sendmessage(DBG_GET_STRING_AT, (void*)addr, text))
        return true;
    return false;
}
```

## Root cause: shutdown race

The dump shows a fully-populated widget tree painting under a live `application.exec()` loop, so `bIsStopped` was `true` because of **shutdown**, not startup (during startup the Qt event loop is not running yet).

The shutdown sequence drives the race:

1. User closes the main window. `MainWindow::closeEvent` shows a **non-modal** "Closing..." dialog, starts a worker thread, and then `event->ignore()`s, so **the main window stays visible and keeps painting**:

   ```cpp
   // src/gui/Src/Gui/MainWindow.cpp:843
   mCloseThread->start();
   emit Bridge::getBridge()->close();
   ...
   else
       event->ignore();   // window not closed yet -> still repainted
   ```

2. The worker thread runs `DbgExit()`:

   ```cpp
   // src/gui/Src/Utils/MainWindowCloseThread.cpp:10
   void MainWindowCloseThread::run()
   {
       DbgExit();                            // _dbg_dbgexitsignal() -> bIsStopped = true (last step)
       Bridge::getBridge()->setDbgStopped(); // GUI flag, set AFTER DbgExit
       emit canClose();
   }
   ```

3. Between the worker setting `bIsStopped = true` and the window actually closing, the GUI thread is still dispatching `WM_PAINT`. A widget paints, calls `DbgGetStringAt`, and trips the assertion.

This is a classic time-of-check/time-of-use race across two threads.

### Architectural gap

The GUI already has a stop flag, `mDbgStopped`, but it only guards messages flowing **engine -> GUI** (`Bridge::processMessage`, `src/gui/Src/Bridge/Bridge.cpp:300`). There is **no symmetric guard** for the **GUI -> engine** direction (the `Dbg*` query functions called from `paintEvent`). It is also set *after* `DbgExit()`, so it would not help even if it were checked.

## Fix options

1. **Make the engine fail safe (recommended, most robust).** In `_dbg_sendmessage`, for the stopped case return `0`/`false` for read-only query messages instead of `__debugbreak()`. Keep the assertion under `#ifdef _DEBUG` if you still want to catch genuine bugs in debug builds. This covers every late query (paints, timers, async repaints, plugins), not just this one path.

2. **Stop the GUI painting before stopping the engine (lowest risk).** `hide()` the main window (or `setUpdatesEnabled(false)`) in `MainWindow::closeEvent` before `mCloseThread->start()`, so hidden widgets stop receiving `paintEvent`s during the dangerous window.

3. **Add a GUI-side "exiting" guard** checked by the `Dbg*` wrappers, set before the worker runs `DbgExit()`.

Recommended: option 1 as the durable fix, optionally combined with option 2.

## Key references

- `src/dbg/_exports.cpp:900` - `_dbg_sendmessage` and the `__debugbreak()` assertion
- `src/dbg/x64dbg.cpp:936` / `983` / `991` - `bIsStopped` lifecycle and `dbgisstopped()`
- `src/bridge/bridgemain.cpp:1422` - `DbgGetStringAt`
- `src/bridge/bridgemain.h:320` - `DBG_GET_STRING_AT` enum value (`0x26`)
- `src/gui/Src/Gui/MainWindow.cpp:843` - `closeEvent` shutdown sequence
- `src/gui/Src/Utils/MainWindowCloseThread.cpp:10` - worker thread running `DbgExit()`
- `src/gui/Src/Bridge/Bridge.cpp:300` - `mDbgStopped` guard (engine -> GUI only)


### Steps to reproduce

1. 
2. 
3. 


### Attachments

_No response_

title: CPU argument widget "Copy Value" copies stack argument address instead of stack argument value
author: Steesha
body: ### Operating System Windows 11 64bit, Build 26200 ### x64dbg Version Apr 20 2026 ### Describe the issue In the CPU argument widget, the `Copy Value` action behaves differently for register arguments and stack arguments. For register arguments, `Copy Value` copies the actual argument value as expected. For stack arguments such as `[rsp+28]`, `Copy Value` copies the value of `rsp+28` itself, i.e. the stack slot address, instead of the value stored at `[rsp+28]`. ### Steps to reproduce 1. With the default x64 fastcall convention, the argument widget may show something like: ```text 1: rcx 7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFC 7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFC 2: rdx FFFFFFFFFFFFA182 FFFFFFFFFFFFA182 3: r8 0000000000000052 0000000000000052 4: r9 0000003F308FFB10 0000003F308FFB10 5: [rsp+28] 0000000000000000 0000000000000000 6: [rsp+30] 0000000000000000 0000000000000000 ``` 2. Right-clicking argument 5 and selecting Copy Value copies the address rsp+28 evaluates to, instead of copying the displayed/dereferenced value at [rsp+28]. ### Expected behavior For a stack argument displayed as [rsp+28], Copy Value should copy the argument value stored at [rsp+28]. Alternatively, if the current behavior is intentional, the menu item could be renamed or another action could be added, for example: - Copy Address - Copy Value - Copy Dereferenced Value ### Source observation In CPUArgumentWidget::refreshData, stack arguments are displayed using [expr], but mArgumentValues stores DbgValFromString(expr) rather than DbgValFromString("[expr]"). Later, Copy Value copies ToHexString(value), so stack arguments copy the address expression value. Related PR that added the copy action: https://github.com/x64dbg/x64dbg/pull/3595 ### Attachments Then, it copys "3F308FF938" Register dumps: ```text RAX : 00000000FEABD538 RBX : 00000000FFFFD538 RCX : 7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFC RDX : FFFFFFFFFFFFA182 RBP : 0000000140007D74 RSP : 0000003F308FF910 RSI : 00000000FFFFD538 RDI : 00000000AAC4A9AD R8 : 0000000000000052 R9 : 0000003F308FFB10 R10 : 00007FF703707004 R11 : 00007FF703790098 R12 : 0000000000000000 R13 : 0000000000000000 R14 : 0000000000000000 R15 : 0000000000000000 RIP : 00007FF7037900A6 RFLAGS : 0000000000000212 ```

title: Permanent highlighting mode does not apply in Trace view
author: Steesha
body: ### Operating System Windows 11 64bit, Build 26200 ### x64dbg Version Apr 20 2026 ### Describe the issue ### Description When `Permanent highlighting mode` is enabled in the settings, the CPU view behaves as expected, but the Trace view does not seem to use permanent highlighting mode. In the CPU view, selected/highlighted tokens remain highlighted permanently until another token is selected. In the Trace view / Trace Browser, the highlighting still behaves like the normal non-permanent mode. ### Expected behavior The Trace view should respect the global `Permanent highlighting mode` setting and keep the selected token highlighted permanently, like the CPU view does. ### Actual behavior The CPU view uses permanent highlighting mode correctly, but the Trace view does not. ### Notes I searched existing issues/PRs for `permanent highlighting mode`, `permanent highlight`, `Trace Browser highlight`, and `trace highlighting`, but did not find an existing report for this behavior. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Open x64dbg. 2. Enable `Permanent highlighting mode` in the settings. 3. Open the CPU view and select/highlight an instruction token, register, or value. 4. Observe that the highlighting is permanent. 5. Open a trace in the Trace view / Trace Browser. 6. Select/highlight a similar token, register, or value. ### Attachments
